header.service: DEMO — UC San Diego Staff Performance Appraisal (SPA)
header.scope: Setting expectations → Signed appraisal filed in personnel file
header.lead: A. Practice Lead, Director of AI Strategy

entries.whatsWrong: Almost all appraisals are completed in the final two weeks before COB June 15, so close and clearance bunch into one window.
entries.evidence: INT-3: "almost everything lands in the last two weeks before June 15"; INT-2 drafting 11 at once
entries.notes: Reads as 'HR is slow' but is really demand arriving all at once.

entries.whatsWrong: Because the self-appraisal is optional, many employees skip it, so the leader drafts without the employee's own account of the year.
entries.evidence: INT-1: "it's optional so a lot of people just don't do it"; INT-2 reconstructs "from memory"
entries.notes: Weakens the evidence base for the rating decision.

entries.where: Stage 5 (Review, comments, and HR clearance); H-02
entries.whatsWrong: Once a draft moves to 'HR Review' the employee cannot see or edit it and no status is surfaced back, so it disappears into a black box.
entries.evidence: INT-1: "sits in 'HR Review' and I literally can't ... see it"; INT-2: "a bit of a black box"
entries.notes: Distinct from the delay itself — this is the loss of visibility.

entries.whatsWrong: Signatures are chased one at a time over email with no routing support, stalling the close.
entries.evidence: INT-2: "I have to chase signatures, which is all email"
entries.notes: Compounds FR-01 right at the deadline.

entries.whatsWrong: Job descriptions are often out of date when standards are set, so standards are written against stale duties and revisited later.
entries.evidence: INT-2 / blueprint backstage: leader must check "whether duties changed"
entries.notes: Upstream cause of a weaker rating basis.

entries.where: Stage 5 (Review, comments, and HR clearance); D-05
entries.whatsWrong: Employees are sometimes given fewer than the five business days they are owed to comment, because the draft arrives close to the deadline.
entries.evidence: INT-1: "one year it was like two days because we were right up against the June deadline"
entries.notes: Policy is ≥5 business days; the crunch erodes it.

entries.where: Stage 4 (Getting the draft and talking it through); D-04
entries.whatsWrong: Leaders with many reports draft a large batch of appraisals in a short window, so quality and attention drop.
entries.evidence: INT-2: "drafting eleven appraisals in a few weeks ... I'm not going to pretend they all get the same attention"
entries.notes: Capacity shortfall directly degrades the rating decision.

entries.where: Stage 2 (Doing the work and gathering evidence); D-03
entries.whatsWrong: When an employee changes roles mid-cycle and the handoff between leaders is missed, they can be evaluated on the wrong scope of work.
entries.whoFeels: Employees who changed jobs, both leaders
entries.evidence: INT-3: "the new leader only evaluates the work in the new role" — but only if the change is caught
entries.notes: Low frequency but high unfairness when it happens.

clusters.name: Deadline crunch
clusters.sharedRoot: The entire cycle is due in one narrow window with no earlier pacing, so drafting, signatures, and clearance all collide near COB June 15.

clusters.name: Opaque, time-squeezed back half
clusters.sharedRoot: Once the draft leaves the employee's hands they lose both visibility and the time they are owed to respond.

clusters.name: Thin evidence base
clusters.sharedRoot: Standards set on stale job descriptions and a frequently-skipped self-appraisal leave the rating resting on reconstruction rather than record.

data.honestAccount: DEMO — the SPA process works, but its honesty is bought late and under pressure. The whole cycle stacks into the two weeks before June 15, where drafting, employee comment time, HR clearance, and signature-chasing all collide; the highest-stakes step — the rating — too often rests on a skipped self-appraisal and a stale job description rather than on a year's record. Once the draft moves to 'HR Review' the employee loses all visibility and, in the crunch, sometimes the five days they are owed to respond. None of this is a system failure so much as a timing-and-visibility failure: the service asks for its most careful judgment at exactly the moment it has the least time and the least information.
